sentences.text: Yeah.
I'm fine.
At any time, I can take a drink?
My name is Eva Kimel-- E-V-A in English.
Was E-W-A in Polish.
And second name is Kimel-- K-I-M-E-L.
My name at birth was Najnudel, which is N-A-J-N-U-D-E-L.
No, I didn't.
No.
I usually don't like to speak about it, but my birthday is June 19, 1931.
I'll be 65 this year.
I was born in Radom.
R-A-D-O-M.
Radom is in central Poland, near Warsaw.
Well, Radom was a fairly large city.
It was about 60,000 inhabitants, if I can remember correctly.
My mother's family was always from Radom.
My father came from a little town, a small town, [?
Ostrowsa-- ?] Ostrowiec, I'm sorry?
Ostrowiec was probably about an hour and a half or two hours from Radom.
Well, my father owned owned a sawmill.
And I think he acquired it with some partners after he got married to my mother.
I understand that those days, parents of a girl had to give a dowry in order for the men to be able to develop some kind of a business or open a business.
And I just heard about it recently, that there were some problems there.
My father fell in love with my mother immediately when he saw her.
But because of the money that my grandparents didn't want to give, there was slight problem.
But then, finally, they settled.
They settled.
And they got married, and lived, for a few years, together.
Well, they made parquet floors.
And the sawmill was more to the east of Radom, was not-- it was more to, more to the east.
It was very close to the Russian border.
So my father really was spending all day-- a lot of time out there.
And he was only a guest in our house, as far as I can remember.
He was only coming home maybe for Saturday and then back.
During the summer, for the-- we used to go there and spend the time with him.
No, the sawmill was in Zawada-- S-- Z-A-W-A-D-A, near [INAUDIBLE], which was a bigger, bigger town.
Because Zawada was really like a village in the middle of a forest.
Because they used to buy the woods, the trees, and you know, process them into parquet floors.
Oh, that was-- that must have been about seven hours, eight hours by train.
That's right.
Well, it was a, I think, a three-- three story building.
It was an apartment building that was really owned by my father's sister.
And we lived on the third floor.
We had the nice two bedroom, as we would call it here, apartment, with a large kitchen, I remember, and small bathroom.
And it was, was a nice apartment for those, you know, for those days, those standards.
Yeah, that was a modern apartment.
You could see. ?]
Oh, yes.
Yes, that's right.
Yes, well, in, in the Polish cities those days, there was no big differentiation because-- between the residential and commercial, like we know it here in the United States.
Everything was really mixed in.
So you could have had stores underneath and businesses.
And like my uncle had business in his apartment on the second floor.
That's the uncle that owned the building.
He had a business right then and there.
So--
He had leather business.
So it really was mixed, but was mostly residential, I would say, because they were, you know, quite nice apartment buildings.
My school was not too far away from, from there.
It was a private school, a private, Jewish school.
So I learned, I remember, I learned about the holidays.
And I learned, I guess, a little Hebrew, but can't remember from those days at all.
But I remember learning about Passover and about asking the four questions.
So those things, I do remember.
Yes, my family was observant.
They were not ultra-religious.
But my, my mother did have a kosher home.
And my grandparents had a kosher home.
And I know that every Saturday, I remember that I was, I was asked to come to dinner to my grandparents.
And even if my mother, my parents didn't go, an uncle always came and picked me up and took me there.
I was the first grandchild.
So I was a big [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, this was my mother's parents.
Now my father's mother was deceased.
I never remembered her.
And her name-- I was named after her.
My grandfather, my father's father, was ultra-orthodox.
So as we will see on the picture that later on, he wore a [? capata ?], and he wore-- he had a beard.

And, and it's very interesting, which I didn't remember and my mother was telling me, that he only ate-- when he came to visit us from Ostrowiec to Radom, he only stayed with us, and only ate in my mother's house.
Because my mother was more kosher, he thought, than his other daughters that lived in Radom.
Well, with me, they spoke Polish.
Amongst themselves, I would say they spoke Yiddish.
In my house, they spoke Polish.
But when they didn't want me to understand, then they spoke Yiddish.
So naturally, I learned how to understand.
Because I wanted to know what's going on.
In my school, the language was Polish.
Yes, but--
I don't think so.
I don't think so.
It had to be.
Because I remember, I was learning about the holidays and about, about Jewishness.
So I guess it had to be.
I don't remember.
It might have been.
Yeah, it probably was.
Not really, because, I guess, we lived in, in a different world, where we went to school with the same type of children.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I don't think that my mother let me, let me down to go downstairs and play with everybody.
Because you know, I was always either with my mother, or with the maid, or with somebody.
So I don't remember if I was playing, you know, with non-Jewish children.
I mean, I remember my very good friends were Jewish.
Well, as far as my memory goes, I don't remember time we got up, but we had breakfast.
And after that, at 10 o'clock every morning, I went downstairs to my aunt, who owned the building.
And there, I had my second breakfast.
Because apparently, I was a little chubby girl.
And I loved to eat.
And after that, we would-- my would probably-- I would go back upstairs.
And we would go to a cafe house with my mother.
And we would have another piece of danish, because I was not allowed to have any other piece of cake because it wasn't good for me-- piece of danish.
My mother would spend, I guess, an hour, two hours there.
And then we would, I guess, go back home.
But this was the days when I didn't have school.
Well, when I had school, then I guess I would be taken, walked to school.
After school, I guess I would come home.
They would bring me back home for dinner.
Because dinner was at-- main meal was at 2 o'clock.
And then I had to do homework.
And I guess, again, I was walked to a friend's house.
Or a friend would come to my house.
And we would play.
Or they would take me to the park, I guess, and for a walk, or to play in the park.
Well, my mother-- my father was in Radom really very little, because his business was really in Zawada.
So he spent most of the time there.
My mother, I doubt it.
Because she, again, had a large family.
And she was very close with, you know, with her parents.
And her parents had a fur, fur business.
So if you had nothing to do, you would go in to the-- to visit your parents and spend a little time there.
My father probably, from the business point of view, probably did have to deal with the Polish people.
No.
Upper, upper middle class, I would say, yes.
No.
No.
I was a little girl, and I-- All I know that my two cousins emigrated to Israel.
And I know that they were Zionists.
And my father helped them financially to, to leave Poland.
Well, they were either celebrated in our house, or most of the holidays were really celebrated in my grandparents' house.
My grandparents had a big family.
There were eight children, of which only one was abroad, was in France, studying medicine.
Everybody else was home.
So when we sat down around the table, there were quite a few of us.
And I remember the holidays, either Friday nights, or Saturday dinners, or other holidays.
It's interesting, because I still remember Passover being celebrated in our house.
And remember that I was the youngest one, so I asked the four questions.
And I opened the doors for the [?
Elijah, ?] [?
Elijah ?] to come in.
Yes.
Not then.
Towards the end of '38-- and I remember vividly.
We were in the Zawada at that time.
That was the summer of '38.
And all of a sudden, I heard people coming and going, and a lot of commotion, talking that a war might break out and whatever.
But somehow, nobody believed in it.
No.
No.
Yes, with, you know, with talking about the war, that the Germany might strike.
And there might be a war.
Yes, Hitler's name was, was mentioned.
And I remember there was Czechoslovakia that he, that he--
Declared war, war.
I think-- and that, I didn't learn then, I learned much later on-- that my father's sister was already at the train station.
And she was going to leave.
And I don't remember who that was, but started crying tremendously, somebody from the family, that she's leaving, and nobody will see her, et cetera.
And she-- at last minute, she changed her mind, so remained in Poland, and was killed.
She was going to Palestine.
Oh, no, excuse me.
She was going to America.
She was going to America.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I, I remember-- I don't remember the minute when it broke out.
But I remember, like, a day or two days later, my father came and said, let's leave.
We are going to Zawada.
So though my mother wasn't so crazy about it because she didn't want to leave her family, he rented a truck and packed us up.
My grand-- my grandparents gave my mother a big-- not case.
What did they call it?
Not suitcase, but a big--
Trunk-- a big trunk of fur she should take with her.
So we loaded everything on the, on the truck.
And we took a cousin with us, who was a daughter of my aunt from the building.
And she left with us.
And I think it was on the 3rd or the 5th of September, 1939 where we left.
And we were, I think, ones of the last ones to leave before Radom was shelled and bombed.
And, and that's how our journey started.
It took us a few days, because we had to stop on the way.
And you know, we couldn't go because there were bombings already.
And there were military soldiers.
And you know, they, they were not letting the private vehicles through.
So it took us quite a few days.
I don't remember exactly how long.
Yeah.
Oh, we had a chauffeur.
Yeah, we were on top of the truck.
It was like an open dump truck, really.
I don't remember.
No, no, no.
We just took the, we just took the personal, probably, personal belongings.
And the fur, I remember, because my mother was talking about it later on.
And we lived.
You know, we lived on it, too.
Because we were-- when we didn't have other means, whatever, my mother was selling it.
And, and that was our income.
No, probably not yet.
Not yet, because we were going east.
And the Germans were coming from the west.
Also, going back, what I remember when the war started that, you know, when there were raids, the windows had to be all taped up so there were not-- the glass was not shattering.
And so that, that I remember.
You know, I don't remember that.
I don't remember.
I guess not.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
All I remember, we got to Zawada.
And we stayed there, maybe a few days, or-- and the Russians were there already, in Zawada.
And my father went to the train station, I guess to see what's going on, and to, to feel, feel the situation, and came home and told my mother, let's go to Russia.
Because he spoke-- he met some Russians there.
And they told him we can get on the train and, and, and go to Russia to live.
But my mother would never do that.
Because she didn't want to leave her family behind.
And she said, no way.
So we stayed there.
And from there, we started-- I guess we had to leave, because-- I don't remember whether that was-- which year that was.
But that had to be '39, probably.
We started moving east.
Did the Germans ever get to Zawada?
Oh, they did.
But we were not there anymore.
We must have stayed maybe a week to two weeks, not-- as far as my recollection, you know?
I don't think it was more than that.
Because the Germans were, you know, moving, moving east.
And we had to leave.
No, not yet.
Not yet.
Not yet.
Not yet.
That was too early yet.
It is possible that my, my father heard of, you know, some things.
And we knew that we would be safer under the, under the Russians than under the Germans.
Nothing.
He just left it.
He--
He just left it.
It Is possible that people were started telling him, you better leave.
Because you know, because you might be killed.
Everybody was running away.
And I guess everybody's feeling was that, that the people-- the Jews were safer under the Russians than under the Germans.
No, Zawada was a little village, really.
No.
No.
Not at all.
Maybe the few Jews that were there were the people that were-- that my father was in business with.
We'd been to quite a few towns.
The, the one of that stands out in my mind the most was Luck-- L-U-C-K.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in Luck, we spent, probably-- we had to spend quite a long time.
Because I remember I went to school in Luck.
And in Luck, there were Russians already.
I don't remember.
But it was a middle-- it was a little town, I guess.
We lived in an apartment, but I, I really-- well, I-- it was, I think, a one-story, a one-story building.
And there was some Russian people that rented the apartment to us.
And I, I went to school there.
And there already, I had Ukrainian friends, and Polish friends, and all kinds of friends.
I remember that behind our apartment there was-- it was a hilly town, I guess.
It had mountains or whatever.
And we used to sleigh ride.
And, you know, for so a year or so, we had a normal life.
My father was working there.
Because, I guess he was experienced in the, in the mill.
So he did get a job.
And, and an uncle, one of my uncles, ran away from, from Radom, and somehow caught up with us.
And he was staying-- he was with us, too.
And, and this was-- and I don't remember.
It might have been 1940.
That was still before the, the outbreak of the Russian-German war.

And one day, somebody-- the Russian people, the Ukranian people that we lived with-- tipped my parents off that the Russians are coming, and that they are taking all the refugees and sending them to Siberia.
And my parents-- now, this I was told.
I don't remember this incident.
But they jumped out the windows.
And they hid in the back of the building in a, like-- what do you call it, where the horses-- in a stable.
And they left the two of us.
Me and my sister, they left home.
So the Russians really came in and asked for my parents.
The people said they didn't know where they were.
So they wanted to take me and my sister.
And the people stood up.
And they said, over their dead bodies, which was really very brave of them.
They didn't know the-- They were Ukrainian?
Right.
They didn't know the Russians at that time yet.
Because they could have been shot right then and there.
But they told them they were not going to give the children away.
They should come back and, and take the whole family.
But she, she wasn't giving the children.
So they, they left.
And after that-- my cousin, also, that we took with us from Radom was with us up to that point.
Now, she told me later on that, from then on, she left.
Because she came back.
And she didn't find us.
So apparently, right after my, my parents came back, they found out about it.
We had to pack and leave.
Because they were afraid that my parents were going to be-- that we are going to be taken, which maybe would have been better.
Maybe my father would have lived if they took us to Siberia.
But who know?
From there, we went to a little town named Ludwipol.
It's spelled L-U-D-W-I-P-O-L.
And was it east?
West?
That was east.
East.
Everything was east.
We were going east.
Everything was east.
We might have been in some little towns all along the way.
But I really cannot recall.
I remember that I used to be in orphanages-- not, maybe not orphanages, but in like daycare center.
And I remember laying on a cot in the afternoon and sleeping.
And I said, well, I'm going to write a book one day, if I survive.
But everything just-- I, I, I can't recall these days.
I mean, I just remember very slight moments and whatever, but not the whole story.
Anyhow, we went to Ludwipol, Poland.
Again, it was a little town.
My father got a job there.
My uncle caught up with us-- another uncle caught up with us, my mother's, well, still younger brother.
My mother was the oldest.
And he was really the-- he was in, in the fur business with my grandfather.
So he started really selling business, and do some selling and going from town to town and trying to make some living.
My father got a job there, again.
And I went to school there, again.
So I really had, like, two years of Russian schooling.
And I remember, it was my birthday on the 19th of June.
And my mother made me a big birthday party.
And I had a lot of Ukrainian friends, some Polish friends, and whatever.
Because by then, life had changed.
And the next day, I woke up.
And everybody started saying the war, the war broke out 20th of June, 1941.
